content:
First observe that the 3×3 grid is a bipartite graph (a “checker‐board”), with one colour‐class of size 5 (the centre and the 4 corners) and the other of size 4 (the 4 edge‐midpoints).  Since there are exactly 5 odd numbers (1,3,5,7,9) and 4 even numbers (2,4,6,8), the only way to make every edge join opposite‐parity labels is to place all 5 odds on the 5‐vertex class and all 4 evens on the 4‐vertex class.  Within those cells we may permute the 5 odds in 5! ways and the 4 evens in 4! ways, giving in total  
   5!·4! = 120·24 = 2880  
valid labellings.

Finally, we identify labellings that differ by a rotation of the whole board.  No nontrivial rotation (90°, 180° or 270°) can fix any of these labellings (because that would force some labels to repeat), so each orbit under the rotation‐group has size 4.  By Burnside’s lemma (or simply dividing by 4) the number of distinct arrangements up to rotation is  
   2880 ÷ 4 = 720.  

Answer: \boxed{720}
